raw_consumer_statement:
This is a follow-up to CFPB Case # XXXX regarding American Expresss handling of fraudulent charges related to XXXX XXXX Marketing / XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX ). 
On XX/XX/XXXX, XX/XX/XXXX, and XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX transactions appeared on American Express statements as : XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX TX The total amount of these transactions was {$24000.00}, split between an Amex Gold Card ( ending XXXX ) and a XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX ( ending XXXX ). 
The merchant advertised a done-for-you business program but never delivered the promised services. The services were misrepresented, and the charges were obtained through fraudulent and deceptive practices. This issue is part of an active civil lawsuit in XXXX XXXX, Texas ( 44th District Court, Case No. XXXX ) alleging deceptive trade practices and fraud. 
A group of XXXX affected consumers have filed formal FTC complaints against this same merchant. The FTC instructs victims to file a forced chargeback with their card issuers under the FTC Holder Rule ( 16 C.F.R. 433 ) and 15 U.S.C. 1666i. Despite this guidance, American Express has refused to comply and continues to rely on the 60-day billing-error rule ( 15 U.S.C. 1666 ), which does not apply to merchant fraud or misrepresentation. 
On XX/XX/XXXX, American Express issued a denial letter ( Ref. XXXX ) stating the charges were too old. This is legally incorrectthe cited statute governs only billing errors and does not limit the Holder Rule or 1666i, which make card issuers jointly liable for merchant fraud, even if accounts are fully paid.

Amex was asked to : Reopen the dispute under 1666i / FTC Holder Rule ; Provide its compliance policy for these laws ; and Refund the {$24000.00} by check or direct deposit ( since both accounts are paid off ). 
The disputed balances were ultimately paid in full, including by using a self-directed IRA to pay off the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. Additional interest, minimum-payment charges, and possible late fees were incurred as a result of this fraudulent transaction. Reimbursement of these costs is requested under 15 U.S.C. 1666i and the FTC Holder Rule, as they are consequential damages directly arising from the misrepresented transactions.

American Express has provided the same 60-day response repeatedly by phone, chat, and in writing.

Requested Action : Escalate this case to CFPB Enforcement / Supervision for review of American Expresss refusal to apply 1666i and the FTC Holder Rule despite FTC guidance instructing consumers to request chargebacks.

Require American Express to issue a substantive written legal response acknowledging its obligations under these laws.

Require American Express to refund the {$24000.00} obtained through misrepresentation, with reimbursement of all related interest and fees caused by the fraudulent transactions.
